text:
The 3,000m steeplechase is one of the most intriguing events at theOlympic Games– and its roots go back further than Queen Victoria's reign. Originating in Ireland in the 18th century, the event has evolved significantly from its early days when horses and riders would race from one town's church steeple to another, navigating various countryside obstacles along the way.

While the horses and rugged terrain may have been replaced by athletes and a track, the steeplechase remains just as chaotic. The event has also been in the games for more than a century but has also seen some changes take place.

As we approach the men's and women's 3,000m steeplechase final,Express Sportdelves into how this unique event operates.

Discovery+is the new home of the Olympics. The streamer is set to broadcast 3,800 hours of sport live from Paris.

Sports fans can getDiscovery+ for £3.99a month until the end of 2024 via Amazon Prime's video channels. This also comes with anAmazon Prime 30-day free trial.

The steeplechase stands out as the only athletics event that incorporates water into the race. It's also the sole distance event that requires competitors to navigate barriers, reportsthe Mirror.

Participants must clear a series of both wet and dry jumps across a 3,000m distance, equivalent to seven-and-a-half laps of the track. In total, there are 28 fixed barriers and seven water jumps to be negotiated, with the latter of these usually placed on a separate part of the track to the main running surface.

Unlike other events, the barriers in the steeplechase are fixed, allowing athletes to step on them and push off, rather than having to hurdle them entirely. For the men's event, these barriers stand at 91.4cm high, while the women's measure 76.2cm.



The steeplechase made its debut at the 1900 games in Paris. It was standardised at 3,000m at the 1920 games in Antwerp, Belgium.

The women's steeplechase was only introduced at the 2008 Olympics in Beijing. While the race's origins can be traced back to Ireland, the Olympic version was also influenced by the renowned two-mile cross-country races at Oxford University during the 19th century.

It became a track event that included barriers at the 1879 English Championships. When it was incorporated into the Olympics, the distances of the races varied, before being standardised at 3,000 metres for Antwerp.



In the women's event, Ugandan sensation Peruth Chemutai, the defending champion, effortlessly advanced to the final in her opening heat. She led from the front and finished a staggering 10.51 seconds ahead of Kenya's Faith Cherotich, the bronze medallist at the 2023 World Championship.

Meanwhile, Kenyan world record holder Beatrice Chekkoech triumphed in her heat, while Bahrain's Winfred Yavi, the 2023 world champion, also progressed to the final. The men's event is set for an electrifying face-off between Moroccan Olympic champion Soufiane El Bakkali and Ethiopian world record holder Lamecha Girma.

El Bakkali clinched gold in Tokyo, but Girma holds the world record, which he established last year. Having settled for silver and finishing behind the Moroccan in Tokyo, revenge will undoubtedly be on his agenda.

title: Olympic 3,000m steeplechase explained and how athletics event works | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Patrick Austen-Hardy
description: The 3,000m steeplechase is a unique event on the athletics schedule, with the men's and women's finals now upon us.
